title: The cool old house
summary: A visitor in Spain learns why an old house with thick walls feels cooler than a new one with air conditioning.
body:
Lucia lives in a small town in Spain. Her house is very old. The walls are thick. They are made of stone and mud. In summer, the sun is very hot. The street is hot. But inside the house, the air is cool. There is no air conditioning.

A visitor came last week. His name was Tom. Tom was from England. He was surprised. He said, 'How is your house so cool? Mine has a machine, and it is still hot!' Lucia smiled. She put her hand on the wall. 'The wall is the secret,' she said. 'It is very thick. The heat takes a long time to go through it. In the day, the wall stops the heat. At night, it is cool, and the house breathes out the warm air.'

Tom touched the wall. It was cool, even at noon. He was happy. He thought a machine was the only way to be cool. But this old house was clever. It used no power at all. The thick wall did the work. Sometimes the old way is the best way, Tom thought.

grammarNotes.title: Past simple
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Dùng 'past simple' cho các hành động đã xong trong quá khứ: 'A visitor came last week.', 'Tom touched the wall.'
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use the past simple for finished past actions: 'A visitor came last week.' and 'Tom touched the wall.'

grammarNotes.title: Comparative adjectives
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Thêm '-er' để so sánh: 'the air is cool' và 'a new one with air conditioning' khác với 'cooler'. Trong bài có 'so cool'.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Add '-er' to compare two things. The summary uses 'cooler', and the body uses 'so cool' to show a strong degree.

translationVi:
Lucia sống ở một thị trấn nhỏ ở Tây Ban Nha. Ngôi nhà của cô rất cũ. Những bức tường rất dày. Chúng được làm bằng đá và bùn. Vào mùa hè, mặt trời rất nóng. Con đường thì nóng. Nhưng bên trong nhà, không khí mát mẻ. Không có máy điều hòa.

Tuần trước có một vị khách đến. Tên anh ấy là Tom. Tom đến từ nước Anh. Anh ấy ngạc nhiên. Anh ấy nói: 'Sao nhà cô mát thế? Nhà tôi có máy mà vẫn nóng!' Lucia mỉm cười. Cô đặt tay lên tường. 'Bức tường là bí mật,' cô nói. 'Nó rất dày. Sức nóng phải mất một thời gian dài mới đi xuyên qua được. Ban ngày, bức tường chặn sức nóng lại. Ban đêm trời mát, và ngôi nhà thở ra không khí ấm.'

Tom chạm vào tường. Nó mát, ngay cả vào buổi trưa. Anh ấy vui. Anh ấy từng nghĩ máy móc là cách duy nhất để mát. Nhưng ngôi nhà cũ này thật khéo léo. Nó không dùng chút điện nào cả. Bức tường dày làm việc đó. Đôi khi cách cũ lại là cách tốt nhất, Tom nghĩ.

options: In a small town in Spain
questions.explanation: 'Lucia lives in a small town in Spain.'

questions.prompt: What are the walls made of?
questions.explanation: 'They are made of stone and mud.'

questions.prompt: Does Lucia's house have air conditioning?
questions.hint: Look at the end of the first paragraph.

options: The house was cool with no machine
questions.explanation: He asks, 'How is your house so cool?'

questions.prompt: What does Lucia call the wall?
questions.explanation: 'The wall is the secret,' she said.

questions.prompt: What does the thick wall do in the day?
questions.explanation: 'In the day, the wall stops the heat.'

options: The house breathes out warm air
questions.explanation: 'At night, it is cool, and the house breathes out the warm air.'
The house is 'clever' because its design keeps it cool with no power.
questions.hint: Think about how the house works.

questions.prompt: How much power does the old house use to stay cool?
What is the main idea of the passage?
options: A thick old wall can keep a house cool with no power
Tom does not like old houses
questions.explanation: The thick wall does the work and uses no power.

questions.prompt: What did Tom think at the end?
options: The old way is the best way sometimes
questions.explanation: 'Sometimes the old way is the best way, Tom thought.'

blanks.explanation: 'She put her hand on the wall.'

criticalThinkingQuestion: Lucia's house stays cool with a thick wall and no machine, while Tom's new house with a machine is still hot. Is the 'newest' tool always the best tool? When might an old, simple idea beat a modern machine?
